expected_response.answer: Gravity is not faster than the speed of light because changes in the gravitational field propagate at exactly the speed of light [d3]. The older Newtonian model assumed gravity acted instantaneously ("action at a distance"), but this has been superseded by modern physics [d3]. Einstein's special theory of relativity establishes that nothing travels faster than the speed of light, a principle that applies to gravitational influences as well [d2]. Therefore, while gravity may seem instantaneous on everyday scales, its changes and waves are fundamentally limited to light speed [d3].

expected_response.answer: Mass and gravity are fundamentally linked: gravity is defined as an interaction between masses, meaning without mass, there is no gravitational interaction [d1]. Gravity pulls anything with mass together, and this is why gravity—despite being weaker than electromagnetism—dominates the large-scale structure of the universe, since mass always accumulates while opposite electric charges cancel out [d3]. According to Newton's theory of gravity, the gravitational force on a particle with zero mass would be zero, which is why massless objects like light were not expected to be affected by gravity under Newtonian physics [d4]. Additionally, when considering extended objects, the external forces of gravity appear to act on the center of mass, with each individual particle being pushed or pulled by gravity according to its mass and position [d5].

Osteoma An osteoma (plural: "osteomata") is a new piece of bone usually growing on another piece of bone, typically the skull. It is a benign tumor. When the bone tumor grows on other bone it is known as "homoplastic osteoma"; when it grows on other tissue it is called "heteroplastic osteoma". Osteoma represents the most common benign neoplasm of the nose and paranasal sinuses. The cause of osteomata is uncertain, but commonly accepted theories propose embryologic, traumatic, or infectious causes. Osteomata are also found in

Rhinolith A rhinolith is a calculus present in the nasal cavity. The word is derived from the roots "rhino-" and "-lith", literally meaning "nose stone". It is an uncommon medical phenomenon, not to be confused with dried nasal mucus. A rhinolith usually forms around the nucleus of a small exogenous foreign body, blood clot or secretion by slow deposition of calcium and magnesium salts. Over a period of time, they grow into large irregular masses that fill the nasal cavity. They may cause pressure necrosis of the nasal septum or lateral wall of nose. expected_response.answer: The bony structure of the nasal cavity is formed by several bones working together. The anterior nasal aperture (piriform aperture) is bounded above by the inferior borders of the nasal bones and laterally by the thin, sharp margins of the maxilla [d3]. The hard palate also contributes as a strongly ossified bony floor associated with the nasal cavity [d2]. Additionally, a bony nasal septum divides the nasal cavity into two halves at the midline, with the maxillae expanding to the sides [d4]. Together, the nasal bones, maxilla, hard palate, and nasal septum collectively form the bony framework of the nasal cavity [d3][d2][d4].

The Rule of Thirds is a genuine compositional guideline in which photographers intentionally place subjects at the intersection points of an imaginary grid dividing the frame into nine equal parts [d1, d4]. The theory holds that placing points of interest at these intersections or along the grid lines produces a more balanced and visually appealing image, rather than centering the subject [d3, d5]. It does not require that centering always be avoided — it simply provides professionals with a unique compositional tool [d2]. The rule is meant to be applied proactively during composition, not retroactively overlaid on random images [d1, d2].
